prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Goal
Title: Build confidence and positive self-image
Description: Help Sam recognize his strengths and feel proud of who he is.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

No research papers available yet. Use general evidence-based therapeutic techniques.

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- Include breath-timing cues for breathing exercises: "Breathe in... two... three... four..."
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- Target duration: 10 minutes (approximately 1200 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm so happy you're here with me today. You know what? You are already doing something really brave. You are listening... and that's a great start. Today, we are going to go on a little adventure. It's an adventure all about you... and all the amazing things that make you, you. We might even do some building along the way. So if you have some LEGO bricks nearby, you can grab a few... any colors you like. And if you don't, that's perfectly okay. We can use our imaginations. Are you ready, Sam? Let's begin...

First, let's get really comfy. You can sit or lie down... whatever feels good. Maybe wiggle your toes a little... and let your shoulders relax. Good. Now, let's take a big, slow breath together. Breathe in through your nose... like you're smelling a yummy flower... one... two... three... And breathe out through your mouth... like you're blowing out birthday candles... one... two... three... four. Let's do that one more time. Breathe in... two... three... and slowly breathe out... two... three... four. Nice job.

Sometimes, Sam, our brains can be like a backpack. We can carry around thoughts that feel heavy. Thoughts like, "I'm not good at this"... or "I made a mistake." Lots of kids have thoughts like that. I do too, sometimes. But today, we are going to look inside a different bag. We are going to look inside your superpower bag. Everyone has one. And we are going to find your special strengths.

Let's start with something fun. I want you to think about your hands. Look at them. Wiggle your fingers. These hands can do so many things. Can you think of one thing your hands are really good at? Maybe it's building an awesome LEGO tower... or drawing a funny picture... or giving a really good high-five. Think of just one thing... Got it? Good. Now, in your mind, I want you to put that thing right into your superpower bag. Imagine it like a shiny, gold LEGO brick. You just added your first strength, Sam.

Now let's think about your heart. Your kind heart. Can you think of a time you were kind? Maybe you shared a toy... or helped someone who was sad... or said something nice to a friend. Even a small kind thing counts. Think of that time... Feel that warm, kind feeling in your heart. That is a superpower too. Not everyone remembers to be kind. But you do. Let's take that kindness... and put it in your bag. Another shiny brick.

Here's another one, Sam. Think about your brave. Being brave doesn't mean you're never scared. It means you try something even when it feels a little wobbly. Like the first time you rode a bike... or said hello to someone new... or tried a food you weren't sure about. Can you think of one brave thing you've done? Big or small... it all counts. That's your courage. Let's pick it up... and put that brave brick right in your bag. Your bag is getting full of good stuff.

Now, if you have some LEGO bricks, let's build something with our hands. If you don't, just pretend with me. Pick up one brick... any color you like. This brick is for something you are... right now. Maybe you are a good listener... or a great storyteller... or someone who loves animals. Think of what you are... and as you click that brick down, say it in your mind. "I am a good friend." Or "I am curious." Good.

Now pick another brick. A different color. This one is for something you love to do. Do you love to run? Or read? Or make people laugh? As you click this brick onto the first one, think about that thing you love. Feel how happy it makes you. That's a part of you too.

Let's keep building, Sam. Add another brick. This one is for something you learned. Maybe you learned how to tie your shoes... or count to a big number... or write your name. Every time you learn something, you grow a little bit. Click that brick on. Your tower is getting taller.

Sometimes, a thought might pop in that says, "But what about the things I'm not good at?" That's okay. Everyone has those. Even the best builders have bricks that don't fit sometimes. What do they do? They don't throw the whole tower away. They just look for a different brick. Or they try building it another way. Your "not yet" skills are just bricks you haven't found the right spot for yet.

Let's take a break from building. Put your hands on your tummy. Let's do our special breathing again. This breath is called "Strong Mountain Breath." Imagine you are a tall, strong, proud mountain. Breathe in... and feel your mountain get tall... one... two... three... four. Hold it... two... three. And breathe out... feeling steady and strong... two... three... four... five. You are that mountain, Sam. Strong and here.

I want to tell you a short story. Once, there was a little builder. She wanted to build the tallest tower in the world. She started with a red brick. Then a blue one. But sometimes, her tower would wobble and fall. She felt frustrated. Then, she looked at all the bricks on the floor. She saw the red one she started with. She saw the blue one. She saw all the colors. And she realized... even when the tower fell, the bricks were still there. Her bricks were still good. She could always start again. And each time, she learned a new way to build. You, Sam, are like that builder. You have all your good bricks. Your kindness brick. Your brave brick. Your "I love to" brick. They are always there. Even on a wobbly day.

Let's finish our building. Look at your LEGO creation or imagine it in your mind. That tower is a picture of you. It's unique. No one else in the whole world has a tower exactly like yours. Because no one else is exactly like you. Can you point to one brick you feel really proud of? Just look at it. That strength is yours forever.

Now, let's put our hands on our hearts. Close your eyes if you want to. Feel your heart beating. That beat is like a quiet drum... saying "I am here... I am here." Inside you is your superpower bag... and your unique tower. You are kind. You are brave. You are a learner. You are you. And that is more than enough.

Before we finish, Sam, I have one little idea. Maybe later, you can show your tower to someone you love. Or tell them about one brick in your superpower bag. You can say, "Hey, guess what? One of my strengths is..." Sharing our strengths makes them feel even more real.

You did such great work today. Remember, Sam, you can visit your superpower bag anytime. Just take a Mountain Breath... and remember one golden brick. Thank you for building with me. Be really proud of yourself. You are amazing, just as you are.

If you ever want to talk more about your superpowers, you can always ask a parent or a grown-up you trust. They love helping you build. Goodbye for now, brave builder.
